title: O(log n): Halving the Problem
subtitle: Why logarithmic time feels almost free

sections.heading: What does 'logarithmic' actually mean?
sections.body:
Take a number and ask: 'how many times do I have to halve it before I get to 1?' That count is **log₂(n)**.

* n = 8 → 8, 4, 2, 1. Three halvings. log₂(8) = 3.
* n = 1024 → 1024, 512, 256, 128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1. Ten halvings. log₂(1024) = 10.
* n = 1,000,000 → about 20 halvings.
* n = 1,000,000,000 → about 30 halvings.

An algorithm that **halves the problem** at each step is O(log n) — and *staggeringly* fast even for huge n.

sections.title: Finding a word in a dictionary
sections.body: Flip to the middle. The word is alphabetically before? Throw away the right half. After? Throw away the left half. Repeat. A 1000-page dictionary needs at most 10 flips to land on the right page.

data.text:
Looking for 23 in [3, 7, 12, 18, 23, 31, 47, 56, 71, 89]

  step 1:  [3, 7, 12, 18, 23, 31, 47, 56, 71, 89]
                            ↑ mid = 31  (too big, go left)

  step 2:  [3, 7, 12, 18, 23]
                   ↑ mid = 12 (too small, go right)

  step 3:           [18, 23]
                     ↑ mid = 18 (too small, go right)

  step 4:               [23]
                         ↑ FOUND at original index 4

  4 steps to search 10 items. For 1,000,000 items: ~20 steps.

sections.heading: The pre-requisite: the data must be sorted
sections.body:
Binary search only works on **sorted** data. If the array isn't sorted, halving tells you nothing: the value could be in either half.

This is the trade-off. Sorting costs O(n log n) up front. After that, you can do thousands of O(log n) lookups for almost nothing. If you'll search the same data many times, sort once.

sections.title: Why is binary search O(log n)?

steps.text: We start with n items in our search window.
After one comparison, the window shrinks to at most n/2.
After another step, n/4. Then n/8. We keep halving.
We stop when the window has at most 1 item left. So we want the smallest k where n / 2ᵏ ≤ 1, which gives k = log₂(n).
steps.highlight: Number of steps ≤ log₂(n). That's the definition of O(log n).

sections.heading: log base doesn't matter (constants drop)
sections.body:
Whether you halve, third-third, or fourth-fourth, all logarithms differ only by a constant factor: `log₂(n) = log₁₀(n) / log₁₀(2)`. So we just write **O(log n)** with no base — the base is absorbed into the constant.

In algorithms, log usually means log base 2, because we're usually halving. In other domains, log might mean ln or log₁₀. The Big O is the same.

sections.caption: How tame log n is, compared to n

data.text:
n          | log₂(n)
  -----------+---------
  10         | 3.3
  1,000      | 10
  1,000,000  | 20
  10⁹        | 30
  10¹²       | 40
  10¹⁵       | 50
  10¹⁸       | 60
  10²¹       | 70

  A binary-search-style algorithm on a trillion items needs 40 comparisons.

questions.question: If binary search on 1,024 items takes 10 comparisons, roughly how many does it take on 2,048 items?
questions.explanation: Doubling the input adds exactly 1 more comparison. That's the magic of log.

questions.question: Binary search requires the array to be:
questions.explanation: Without sorting, halving gives no information — the target could be in either half.

questions.question: What does this print?
questions.explanation: Halving 1024 takes 10 steps to reach 1. log₂(1024) = 10.

questions.question: Iterative binary search has O(__) space; recursive has O(__) space.
questions.explanation: Iterative uses constant variables; recursive adds a stack frame per level (log n deep).

sections.prompt: Write a `firstOccurrence(arr, target)` that returns the **first** index where target appears in a sorted array (with possible duplicates), or -1 if not present. Must run in O(log n).
hints: Plain binary search returns the FIRST hit it encounters — that's not necessarily the leftmost.
When arr[mid] === target, save mid as a candidate answer, but keep searching the left half by setting hi = mid - 1.
If you exit the loop with ans still -1, the target wasn't there.
